name: Uraeus
description: The Uraeus is a stylized, upright form of an Egyptian cobra (Naja haje) used as a symbol of sovereignty, royalty, deity and divine authority in ancient Egypt. It is one of the most important symbols of the pharaohs, representing the goddess Wadjet, one of the earliest Egyptian deities, who often was depicted as a cobra. Wadjet was the patron and protector of Lower Egypt, and upon unification with Upper Egypt, she became one of the joint protectors and patrons of all of Egypt. The Uraeus was believed to possess immense power, capable of spitting fire and venom at enemies, thus protecting the pharaoh and the kingdom from harm. It was typically worn on the brow of the pharaoh's headdress, often alongside the vulture representing Nekhbet, the patron goddess of Upper Egypt. The Uraeus symbolizes the pharaoh's legitimacy and divine right to rule, as well as their ability to command and protect their people. It is a potent emblem of royal power, protection, and the connection between the earthly ruler and the divine realm. The Uraeus also appears in various forms of Egyptian art and architecture, reinforcing its significance in their culture and beliefs.
subtitle: Cobra emblem of royal power
origin: Symbolic representation of the goddess Wadjet

keyMyths.title: Wadjet's Protection
keyMyths.description: Wadjet, as the Uraeus, protected the pharaohs by spitting fire at their enemies. This symbolized the pharaoh's divine right and power to defend Egypt.

keyMyths.title: Unification of Egypt
keyMyths.description: The Uraeus, representing Lower Egypt, joined with the vulture (Nekhbet) of Upper Egypt to symbolize the unified kingdom and the pharaoh's rule over both.

sources.author: George Hart
